seed: vulnerability vs strength; light_vs_weight; mirror_broken; sweep; constellation against tide; closed_corners; thin_vs_heavy; horizon; safety_exposure | abstract variation request 405; target batch count 500
title: Exposure vs. Shelter
reasoning: This artwork draws from the emotional tension of vulnerability and strength, represented by a soft, exposed center and a dense base. The palette of clean white and ice blue creates a sense of calm that contrasts with the striking warmth of warning orange, illustrating the interplay between safety and exposure. The layout features one corner overloaded with color, resulting in an unsettling density that reacts against a backdrop of negative space. This collision occurs at the upper left, creating a dynamic movement from the vibrant colors toward the quieter sections, symbolizing a 'sweep' of emotions that both overwhelms and provides safety. The broken pixels introduce intentional imperfections, echoing the fragility of strength. The pattern combines glitch elements with flood-like swathes, illustrating this complex relationship.

layout.name: pressure_corner
layout.description: One corner is overfilled and the rest of the square reacts with spreading marks.

composition.name: exposure_vs_shelter
composition.idea: A pale exposed center is either protected by a frame or left open to incoming marks.

referenceExample.title: Held Hunger
referenceExample.pattern: red orbit presses against a muted locked border

generationRules: Pick 1 pattern type for emotion A.
Pick 1 pattern type for emotion B.
Pick 1 shape seed as the central object.
Apply 1 to 6 broken pixels to create imperfection.
Leave at least 10 percent negative space unless the mood is panic, rage, urgency, euphoria, or pressure.
Use the shape seed as an abstract organizing metaphor, not as a literal icon.
Make the two selected pattern types visibly collide, overlap, interrupt, or trade space.
Prefer 4 to 8 distinct hex colors, with at least one quiet field color and one accent color.
Keep the 8x8 grid readable at thumbnail size by preserving clear large-scale structure.
Let one emotion dominate 55 to 70 percent of the square unless the selected layout demands balance.
Use broken pixels as intentional disruptions, scars, repairs, leaks, or false echoes.
Avoid perfectly even distribution unless the chosen composition is control, serenity, clarity, or certainty.
Give the main collision a location: center, edge, corner, diagonal, border, or channel.
generationSpec.negativeSpaceRule: Leave at least 10 percent negative space unless the chosen emotional tone needs dense pressure.
